sections.title: Adjective Agreement in Gender, Number, and Case
sections.explanation: Greek adjectives must agree with the noun they describe in gender, number, and case — they change their ending to match. The most common adjective pattern is -ος/-α/-ο (masculine/feminine/neuter). Masculine adjectives end in -ος, feminine in -η or -α, and neuter in -ο. For example, 'καλός' (good) becomes 'καλή' in the feminine and 'καλό' in the neuter. In a noun phrase, every word — article, adjective, and noun — carries the same gender: ο καλός φίλος (the good friend, masculine), η καλή φίλη (the good friend, feminine), το καλό παιδί (the good child, neuter). This agreement is essential in Greek and applies to all adjectives.
sections.tip: Learn each adjective as a trio: καλός / καλή / καλό. The article tells you the gender: ο = masculine, η = feminine, το = neuter.

exercises.prompt: Which form of 'καλός' (good) goes with 'το παιδί' (the child, neuter)?
exercises.explanation: Neuter nouns use the neuter adjective form ending in -ο: το καλό παιδί. Masculine uses -ος, feminine uses -η.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'η ___ γυναίκα' (the beautiful woman — use ωραίος)
exercises.explanation: 'γυναίκα' is feminine (η). The feminine form of ωραίος is ωραία: η ωραία γυναίκα.

exercises.prompt: How does a Greek adjective change when used with different nouns?
options: It changes ending to match the noun's gender, number, and case
It always stays the same form
It only changes for plural nouns
exercises.explanation: Greek adjectives agree with the noun in gender (masculine/feminine/neuter), number (singular/plural), and case. A single adjective has multiple forms.

sections.title: Common Greek Adjectives
sections.explanation: Here are ten of the most useful Greek adjectives, each given in its masculine dictionary form (-ος). Remember that the feminine changes to -η or -α and the neuter to -ο. μεγάλος (big), μικρός (small), καλός (good), κακός (bad), ωραίος (beautiful), νέος (young/new), παλιός (old), ακριβός (expensive), φθηνός (cheap), δυνατός (strong). Learn each with a sample sentence: ο καλός δάσκαλος (the good teacher), ο κακός καιρός (the bad weather), ένα μεγάλο σπίτι (a big house), ένα μικρό παιδί (a small child), ένας νέος φίλος (a new/young friend).
sections.tip: Learn opposites together: μεγάλος/μικρός, καλός/κακός, ακριβός/φθηνός, νέος/παλιός, δυνατός/αδύνατος.

exercises.explanation: 'μικρός' means small — the direct opposite of μεγάλος (big).
'ωραίος/ωραία/ωραίο' means beautiful or nice-looking. 'καλός' means good, 'δυνατός' strong, 'νέος' young/new.

sections.title: Adjective Position, Predicate Use, and Comparatives
sections.explanation: In Greek, adjectives normally come BEFORE the noun, just like in English: ο καλός δάσκαλος (the good teacher), η μεγάλη πόλη (the big city). The article is repeated or placed between the adjective and the noun: ο δάσκαλος ο καλός (a more emphatic or literary word order also exists). Adjectives can also be used predicatively — after the verb 'to be' (είναι): ο δάσκαλος είναι καλός (the teacher is good), η πόλη είναι μεγάλη (the city is big). In the predicate position, the adjective still agrees in gender and number with the subject. To form comparatives in Greek, simply place 'πιο' (more) before the adjective: πιο μεγάλος (bigger), πιο ακριβός (more expensive), πιο ωραία (more beautiful). The superlative adds the definite article: ο πιο μεγάλος (the biggest).
sections.tip: Adjective before noun = attributive (ο καλός φίλος). Adjective after είναι = predicate (ο φίλος είναι καλός). Comparative = πιο + adjective.

examples.en: the good teacher explains (attributive — adjective before noun)
the teacher is good (predicate — adjective after είναι)
the city is big (predicate adjective agrees with feminine subject)

examples.el: αυτό το εστιατόριο είναι πιο φθηνό
examples.en: this restaurant is cheaper (comparative with πιο)

examples.el: η Αθήνα είναι η πιο μεγάλη πόλη
examples.en: Athens is the biggest city (superlative with article + πιο)

exercises.prompt: Where does a Greek attributive adjective normally appear?
exercises.explanation: Greek adjectives normally precede the noun, like in English: ο καλός φίλος (the good friend), η μεγάλη πόλη (the big city).

exercises.prompt: How do you form the comparative 'more expensive' in Greek?
exercises.explanation: The most common comparative in Modern Greek uses 'πιο' + adjective: πιο ακριβός (more expensive), πιο μεγάλος (bigger).

exercises.prompt: Which sentence uses a predicate adjective correctly?
exercises.explanation: In the predicate use, the adjective follows the verb 'είναι' and agrees with the subject: Το βιβλίο είναι ωραίο — neuter subject, neuter adjective form ωραίο.
